worth_it_explanation: Creative blocking decides which advertisers and content categories are allowed to serve against a publisher's video and CTV inventory in FreeWheel, so it sits between the demand a publisher accepts and the standards the publisher and any content owner have agreed to hold. A block list that does not match those standards lets unwanted creatives through, and a block list that is broader than it needs to be removes demand that could have served safely. The check is a review of the block settings already configured in FreeWheel against the publisher's documented standards, so it costs reading and comparison time rather than rebuild time, and the finding points to a concrete change to the block list a publisher can make.
intro: FreeWheel serves video and Connected TV (CTV) ads for publishers and channel owners, and its advertiser-domain and category block controls decide which creatives are eligible to run against that inventory. This subcategory is about whether those block settings match the publisher's own brand-safety standards and any requirements a content owner has placed on the inventory, and whether the blocks are scoped so they suppress the demand they should without cutting into demand that could serve safely. These checks confirm that the block list is a deliberate reflection of documented standards rather than a default carried forward or a broad setting that removes revenue without anyone noticing.

audit_checks.title: Advertiser and category block lists do not match the publisher's brand-safety standards
audit_checks.impact_rationale: When the advertiser-domain and category blocks configured in FreeWheel do not match the publisher's documented brand-safety standards or a content owner's requirements, creatives that should be excluded can serve against premium video inventory. On a broadcaster or channel owner's CTV inventory, a single off-standard creative running next to owned content carries reputational cost and can breach a content-owner agreement, so the gap between the configured blocks and the agreed standards is the risk.

educate.base:
FreeWheel lets a publisher block creatives in two main ways. The first is by advertiser domain, where the publisher lists the domains of advertisers whose creatives should not serve, for example a competitor of a content owner or a brand the publisher will not run. The second is by content category, where the publisher blocks whole categories of creative, for example alcohol, gambling, or political, that fall outside what the inventory should carry. Both controls decide which demand is eligible to win an impression on the publisher's video and Connected TV (CTV) inventory.

The audit question is whether what is configured matches what the publisher actually requires. A publisher's brand-safety standards usually live in a document, a content-owner agreement, or a network policy, and they name the advertisers and categories that may not run. The block list in FreeWheel is the place those standards get enforced. When the two drift apart, the standards say one thing and the inventory serves another, so a creative the publisher meant to exclude can still win an impression next to owned or licensed content when no matching block is configured.

The drift usually comes from the list being set once and not revisited. Standards change as a publisher signs new content-owner deals or updates its own policy, advertisers spin up new domains, and category definitions shift, so a block list that matched the standards a year ago can fall behind without any single change being noticed. The block list is also where the standards-based category mechanics meet an industry vocabulary since category blocking leans on a shared taxonomy of content and advertiser categories rather than free text.

A few signs point to a block list that has drifted from the standards:
  • The advertiser-domain and category blocks in FreeWheel have no documented standard, content-owner agreement, or policy they map back to
  • The block list has not been reviewed against the current standards within a defined window, e.g., a quarter
  • A creative that the publisher's standards name as off-limits has served, or could serve, with no matching block configured to stop it
  • Content-owner requirements that name specific advertisers or categories are not reflected in the block settings for the inventory they cover
You may want to confirm that the configured blocks match the publisher's current brand-safety standards and any content-owner requirements. One approach is to put the documented standards and the configured block list side by side and record every advertiser or category that one names and the other does not.

site_type_overlays.publishing: For a broadcaster or streaming publisher, the inventory often carries owned or licensed programming, and content-owner agreements can name advertisers or categories that may not run against specific shows or channels. You may want to check the block list against each content-owner agreement that applies, not just the publisher's own standard, so the blocks reflect every requirement on the inventory rather than a single house policy.
site_type_overlays.ecommerce: For a commerce-content publisher running shoppable or product-adjacent video, the standards often turn on competitive separation, where a creative from a competing brand running next to a product feature can undercut a commercial relationship. You may want to confirm that the advertiser-domain blocks cover the competitors named in any commerce or sponsorship agreement that governs the video inventory.

educate.learn_more_note: FreeWheel's detailed configuration documentation for advertiser-domain and category blocking is in the partner portal (login required).

investigate.intro: Review the advertiser-domain and category blocks configured in FreeWheel against the publisher's documented brand-safety standards and any content-owner agreements, and record every advertiser or category that one names and the other does not.

freewheel_console.label: FreeWheel console
steps: Open the advertiser and category blocking settings in FreeWheel and list the blocked advertiser domains and blocked content categories currently configured.
Note the scope of each block, for example whether it applies network-wide or to specific inventory, channels, or content owners.
Gather the publisher's brand-safety standards document and any content-owner agreements that name advertisers or categories that may not run.
Compare the configured blocks against the documented standards and record every advertiser or category named in one but missing from the other, along with the inventory it covers.

freewheel_reporting.label: FreeWheel reporting
steps: Build a report of served creatives by advertiser and by creative category for a recent period.
Check the served advertisers and categories against the publisher's standards to see whether any creative that the standards name as off-limits has run.
Record any served advertiser or category that the standards exclude but the block list does not currently cover.

evaluate.logic: The practitioner records the configured advertiser-domain and category blocks alongside the publisher's documented brand-safety standards and content-owner requirements, then notes every advertiser or category that the standards name but the block list does not cover. A block list that reflects the current standards and every applicable content-owner requirement passes. A block list with one or more gaps where the standards exclude an advertiser or category that no configured block covers is a fail recorded with the missing entries and the inventory they affect.
evaluate.fallback_message: Record the configured blocks and the documented standards above, noting any advertiser or category the standards exclude that no block covers, to see the evaluation.

generate.narrative_template: {{voice_found}} that the advertiser-domain and category blocks configured in FreeWheel for {{site_name}} do not match the documented brand-safety standards and content-owner requirements that govern the inventory. The block list is where those standards get enforced, so a gap between the two means a creative the standards exclude can still win an impression against the video and Connected TV (CTV) inventory, which carries reputational cost and can breach a content-owner agreement. {{voice_recommend}} putting the documented standards and the configured block list side by side, recording every advertiser or category one names and the other does not, and adding the missing blocks so the configuration reflects the current standards. After the change, recheck served creatives against the standards to confirm no excluded advertiser or category is still serving.

audit_checks.title: Block lists are broader than the standards require and suppress eligible demand
audit_checks.impact_rationale: A block list scoped wider than the publisher's standards require removes advertisers and categories that could have served safely, so demand that would have competed for an impression is excluded before the auction. The cost is unfilled or lower-priced impressions on inventory the standards would have allowed, which is a revenue loss that no single setting flags because the suppressed demand simply never appears.

educate.base:
Blocking is a trade. Every advertiser domain and content category a publisher blocks in FreeWheel removes demand that would otherwise compete for an impression, which is the point when the demand violates the publisher's standards. The trade turns against the publisher when the block is broader than the standards require. In that case it removes demand that could have served safely and the inventory loses the price that demand would have paid.

Over-broad blocking takes a few shapes. A whole category can be blocked when the standard only excludes part of it, for example blocking all of a content category when the agreement names a narrower slice. A block applied network-wide can cover inventory the standard never meant to touch, for example a content-owner restriction on one channel applied to every channel. Block lists can also accumulate, where domains and categories added for a campaign or a one-off concern stay on the list long after the reason has passed, so the list grows and never shrinks.

The cost is hard to see because suppressed demand leaves no trace in the way a blocked-and-logged creative does. The impression either goes unfilled or clears at a lower price than the removed demand would have paid, and nothing in the report names the advertiser that never got to bid. That is what makes an over-broad block list worth a deliberate review rather than a setting a publisher trusts because it errs on the safe side.

A few signs point to blocks that are broader than the standards require:
  • A content category is blocked in full where the documented standard excludes only a narrower part of it
  • A block applies network-wide where the standard or content-owner requirement covers only specific inventory, channels, or shows
  • Advertiser domains or categories remain blocked with no current standard or agreement that calls for them, often left from a past campaign or concern
  • Fill rate or average price on a slice of inventory runs below comparable inventory, and the difference traces to demand removed by a block rather than to thin demand
You may want to confirm that each block traces to a current standard and is scoped no wider than that standard requires. One approach is to review the block list entry by entry, retire any block with no current basis, and narrow any block whose scope reaches past the inventory the standard covers.

site_type_overlays.publishing: For a broadcaster or streaming publisher, a content-owner restriction often applies to specific shows or channels rather than the whole network, so a block applied network-wide can suppress demand on inventory the agreement never covered. You may want to scope each content-owner block to the inventory the agreement names, so the rest of the network keeps the demand it can serve safely.
site_type_overlays.ecommerce: For a commerce-content publisher, competitive blocks set for one sponsorship or product relationship can outlive the deal that called for them, leaving competitor domains blocked across video inventory after the agreement has ended. You may want to tie each competitive block to a current agreement and retire the blocks whose agreements have lapsed.

educate.learn_more_note: FreeWheel's detailed documentation for the scope and inheritance of advertiser and category blocks is in the partner portal (login required).

investigate.intro: Review each advertiser-domain and category block configured in FreeWheel against the standard that calls for it, checking whether the block traces to a current basis and whether its scope reaches past the inventory the standard covers.

freewheel_console.label: FreeWheel console
steps: List the blocked advertiser domains and blocked content categories configured in FreeWheel, along with the scope each block applies to.
For each block, identify the current standard, content-owner agreement, or policy that calls for it, and flag any block with no current basis on record.
For each block that does trace to a standard, compare its scope against what the standard covers and flag any block applied to inventory the standard never named.
Record the blocks with no current basis and the blocks scoped wider than their standard requires.

freewheel_reporting.label: FreeWheel reporting
steps: Build a report of fill rate and average price by inventory slice, and compare slices that carry heavier blocking against comparable slices that carry less.
Note any slice where fill rate or average price runs below comparable inventory in a way that traces to demand a block removed rather than to thin demand.
Record the inventory where over-broad blocking appears to be costing fill or price.

evaluate.logic: The practitioner records each configured block with the current standard that calls for it and the scope it applies to, then notes any block with no current basis and any block scoped wider than its standard requires. A block list where every block traces to a current standard and is scoped to the inventory that standard covers passes. A block list with blocks that have no current basis, or blocks whose scope reaches past the inventory the standard covers, is a fail recorded with those blocks and the inventory where eligible demand is being suppressed.
evaluate.fallback_message: Record each block with the standard that calls for it and its scope above, flagging any block with no current basis or a scope wider than its standard requires, to see the evaluation.

generate.narrative_template: {{voice_found}} that the advertiser-domain and category blocks configured in FreeWheel for {{site_name}} are broader than the brand-safety standards require, so demand that could have served safely is removed before it can compete for an impression. Blocking is a trade, and a block scoped wider than its standard, or one left from a past campaign, removes demand without protecting anything, which leaves impressions unfilled or clearing below the price the removed demand would have paid. {{voice_recommend}} reviewing the block list entry by entry, retiring any block with no current standard or agreement behind it, and narrowing any block whose scope reaches past the inventory the standard covers. After the change, recheck fill rate and average price on the affected inventory to confirm the eligible demand has returned.
